claims.claim_text: Byte-Pair Encoding builds subword tokens by iteratively merging frequent adjacent pieces.
claims.span_hint: line 110 | section: How Tokenizers Learn Useful Fragments
claims.rationale: The Hugging Face tokenizer summary directly explains that BPE starts with small units and repeatedly merges the most frequent adjacent pair.

claims.claim_text: WordPiece is associated with BERT-family models and uses continuation prefixes such as ##.
claims.span_hint: line 124 | section: How Tokenizers Learn Useful Fragments
claims.rationale: The Hugging Face WordPiece page explicitly describes the ## continuation prefix and explains the algorithm in BERT-style tokenization.

claims.claim_text: SentencePiece works on raw text and represents spaces with ▁ instead of requiring separate pre-tokenization.
claims.span_hint: line 134 | section: How Tokenizers Learn Useful Fragments
claims.rationale: The tokenizer summary directly states that SentencePiece treats input as raw text and includes the space character as ▁ in the vocabulary.

claims.claim_text: English is especially optimized because of English-heavy training data, while other scripts may require more tokens for the same meaning.
claims.span_hint: line 93 | section: Text Becomes Model Input Only After Tokenization
claims.anchor_text: English text is often highly optimized in models trained heavily on English web data. Other scripts may require more tokens for the same meaning, especially when the tokenizer falls back to smaller fragments or byte-level representations.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Token counts can vary substantially across languages and scripts, so teams should measure multilingual inputs with the target tokenizer instead of estimating from English examples.
claims.rationale: The sentence makes a broad causal claim about training data and cross-language token costs that is too sweeping for a single clean source.

claims.claim_text: A rough planning heuristic is that one token is about four English characters.
claims.span_hint: line 200 | section: Measuring Tokens, Cost, and Context Pressure
claims.rationale: OpenAI's help article directly gives this heuristic, but `help.openai.com` is not on the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: UTF-8 encodes Unicode characters using one to four bytes.
claims.span_hint: line 309 | section: Edge Cases That Break Naive Text Processing
claims.rationale: RFC 3629 is the primary UTF-8 specification and explicitly defines UTF-8 as encoding characters in sequences of 1 to 4 octets.

claims.claim_text: Some tokenizers operate partly at the byte level rather than only at the word or character level.
claims.span_hint: line 309 | section: Edge Cases That Break Naive Text Processing
claims.rationale: The tokenizer summary directly documents byte-level BPE and explains that it uses byte values as the base vocabulary.

claims.claim_text: Researchers have documented glitch tokens or anomalous tokens that can trigger strange model behavior.
claims.span_hint: line 326 | section: Edge Cases That Break Naive Text Processing
claims.rationale: This paper explicitly studies glitch tokens and describes them as anomalous tokenizer outputs that can compromise model response quality.

claims.claim_text: Subword tokenization was a major advance for handling rare and unseen words.
claims.rationale: Sennrich et al. is the canonical primary source for using subword units to handle rare and unknown words.

claims.claim_text: Whitespace can be part of a token, and leading spaces are often grouped with the following word.
claims.rationale: The OpenAI cookbook example shows tokens like " is" and " great", directly supporting the claim that leading spaces are often grouped into tokens.

claims.claim_text: Token counts are model-specific because different models can use different encodings.
claims.rationale: The cookbook explicitly states that different models use different encodings, which directly supports model-specific token counts.

further_reading.title: Neural Machine Translation of Rare Words with Subword Units
further_reading.why_relevant: Classic primary source for why subword units matter for rare and unseen words.

further_reading.title: SentencePiece: A Simple and Language Independent Subword Tokenizer and Detokenizer for Neural Text Processing
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary reference for raw-text subword tokenization and whitespace-aware tokenization.

further_reading.title: Hugging Face Tokenization Algorithms Summary
further_reading.why_relevant: Compact comparative reference for BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece, byte-level BPE, and tokenization trade-offs.

further_reading.title: How to Count Tokens with tiktoken
further_reading.why_relevant: Practical reference for measuring tokens, understanding encodings, and seeing whitespace-inclusive token examples.

notes: Excluded code fences, Mermaid, quiz answers, hands-on exercise steps, and non-factual teaching prose per pipeline rules. Most tables were also excluded because they are instructional rather than sourceable factual claims. One heuristic claim uses `help.openai.com` and is therefore marked `needs_allowlist_expansion`.
